So it could be comfort with a laptop, it could be comfort with a microwave, it could be comfort with being bald and you could have a coaching program. And there's somewhere, there's a bunch of people who will pay a lot of money for that. And again, her language is important. It's not so much just about teaching them how to use it, but making them feel good about it. So she's selling more than the technical competence. Let's jump over to page 25. Third big, big thing of the day is success in the field requires a willingness to play the game. Meaning if you are absolutely determined to let people make buying and retention decisions on their own through logic based on the core values you deliver, you are not going to make a lot of money. You're going to have a lot of trouble keeping clients and you're probably not going to have as much fun as you could have otherwise. If you view it as a game you are willing to play and you view it all as a performance, first of all, you think of it that way, you'll think about it better. Then here's the criteria for success. One is the performance has to delight the audience. Now, again, that's all about their values, not yours. So let's take coaching. If we put 10 billionaires in the group, you're going to have slow students and you're going to have champions, right? So no matter what you do, billionaires, non-billionaires, you're going to have slow students and you're going to have champions. You are going to have somebody in coaching, a coaching group, or even in a consulting relationship who it seems to you didn't go in anywhere. Like at the end of the year, they're in the same place they were before. But see, that's your value trying to be imposed on them that they should have gotten somewhere. That's not up to you. It isn't up to you to define it. It isn't up to you to impose it. It isn't up to you to judge them by it. It's only important that they are delighted. That's all that is important. So whatever values they got, if they're happy, that's all that is important. And different people are delighted by different things. I had somebody in coaching group for three years. The only reason this person was there was to show off. That's it. Three years, never ask a question. Took his turn, show what he was doing. He was brilliant. On to the next person. Good. Saves time for somebody else. I get to work less. I don't have to think as hard. He's delighted. Fine. That's what it's about. I wouldn't be delighted. It's just like I just said on the break. If I paid 10,000 bucks to be in a seminar, I'd be running back from the bathroom. Tapping my watch. Hey, Kennedy, let's go. Let's rock and roll here. But if you guys want to stroll back, who am I to impose my value system on you? I can skip some pages, save voice. No matter to me. It's okay by me. And it's not up to me to determine what's going to delight you, other than to make sure I try and delight everybody. So if you view it as performance, then its obligation is to delight the audience and enrich you. That's its two obligations. So everything else is gravy. And people will get what they're going to get. So are you willing to play the game? Are you willing to manipulate people? Are you willing to take control of people? Are you willing to only entertain those who wish only to be entertained? In other words, are you willing to crawl inside there and find out what it is that really is going to make him delighted and figure out a way to get it done?
